entry.title: Issue with SAP Build Apps Booster – Cloud Identity Services Subscription Failed
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

While trying to run the SAP Build Apps booster in my trial subaccount, I am consistently facing an issue at the step where it attempts to subscribe to Cloud Identity Services. The booster shows “failed” at this stage, and as a result I am unable to subscribe to SAP Build Apps successfully.

I have attached screenshots for reference to show the error message and the booster status.

Has anyone else faced this issue, and is there a recommended solution or workaround? Any guidance on how to resolve the Cloud Identity Services subscription failure would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!


SAP Build 
SAP Build Apps 

Even while trying to Install Cloud Identity Service as explained in this blog:  https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/build-apps-trial.html
I still face the subscription failed error.

@Dan_Wroblewski 
@maria_kondratyeva 
@BorisZarske

entry.title: SAP IBP’s HPA 2026 Strategy: Unified Planning and Empowered Adoption
entry.content:
One Unified Planning Area for End-to-End Supply Chain Planning

Harmonised Planning Area (HPA) eliminates the need for separate planning areas (such as the older SAPIBP1 or SAP7F templates) by providing a single, standardised data model for all major IBP modules: Demand, Sales & Operations Planning, Inventory, Response & Supply (order-based planning), etc. Key goals and benefits of the HPA include:

Unified Data Model & Master Data

  •              End-to-End Planning Visibility
  •              Streamlined Integration: Lower TCO & Simplified Maintenance
  •              Improved Performance and New Features

 Why is HPA relevant to customers?

For IBP customers, HPA means faster value realization of a full end-to-end planning process. A single, pre-delivered template (I_SAPIBP2) can cover an integrated business planning process.

Empowering Customers and Partners for HPA Success

To ensure IBP customers and partners can confidently adopt the HPA, SAP has rolled out a comprehensive enablement program as part of the 2025–2026 strategy. This includes:

  •  Early Adopter Care (EAC) Program: Alongside the initial HPA release, SAP launched an HPA Early Adopter Care program (starting around the IBP 2508 release in late 2025) to support customers in implementing HPA and provide them with direct access to SAP product experts
  • Partner Enablement Workshops: SAP recognized that consulting partners play a critical role in scaling HPA to the broader market. In mid/late 2025, SAP organized a series of onsite HPA workshops for partners and SAP consultants in global locations (Walldorf, Dallas, Bangalore, etc.)
  •  Online Help Documentation: SAP has updated its official Help documentation to support HPA.
    • Integrated Business Planning Based on I_SAPIBP2 | SAP Help Portal
  • Field and Sales Enablement: Internally, SAP’s field organizations (consultants, pre-sales, support) have been equipped with HPA knowledge so they can better assist customers.

HPA Adoption Paths and Timeline

The HPA 2026 strategy distinguishes between greenfield, brownfield and blue field adoption scenarios, ensuring a clear path for each.

  •  Greenfield: For new IBP customers or new implementations by existing customers, the strategy is straightforward – start on HPA from day one. Since the 2508 release, all the building blocks for a full end-to-end planning process are available in the harmonized area (I_SAPIBP2), and as of IBP 2508 (mid-2025) SAP even set HPA as the default planning area for new IBP installations (with an option to opt out if necessary). Greenfield projects can directly implement IBP using the latest best-practice content on HPA. This approach is ideal for companies that are new to IBP or those who wish to reimplement to achieve a cleaner, standardized design. By using HPA from the start, greenfield adopters ensure they are aligned with SAP’s roadmap and won’t need a future migration – they’re already on the strategic platform.
  • Brownfield: For existing IBP customers with live systems (whether using the time-series planning area SAPIBP1, order-based planning areas SAP7 and SAP7F, or both in parallel), the strategy to a new I_SAPIBP2 planning area involves a transition plan based on tool-enabled consulting services available from SAP and partners. Initially, customers could experiment with HPA in a sandbox (for example, by copying live data into an HPA test system) while continuing to run their business on the current planning areas. As migration tools mature in 2026, brownfield projects can proceed in earnest. The MOVE2H tool (2605 release) will enable SAP to convert SAP7F order-based planning areas to I_SAPIBP2. Subsequent tool updates will handle SAP7 and SAPIBP1 time-series planning area migration by SAP and partners. Brownfield adoption may involve running HPA in parallel to the previous planning area for some period (to validate results and train users) before cutting over.
  • Bluefield: When merging legacy planning area configuration into I_SAPIBP2, some customers may choose a blue field approach – such as standing up a new productive HPA planning area in parallel to the previous one(s) and gradually phasing over configuration and processes. While more manually intensive, this approach allows planning processes to be redesigned, especially harmonized supply planning. The 2026-2027 SAP IBP strategy supports these flexible approaches by providing the necessary tools and guidance for partial migrations, data syncing, and co-existence of old and new planning areas during transition.

 

In summary, SAP’s migration tooling is designed to facilitate both ‘Brownfield’ and ‘Bluefield’ adoption paths for HPA. Brownfield migrations (upgrading an existing IBP instance to a new I_SAPIBP2 instance) will be possible with MOVE2H and related tools, preserving much of the investment in configuration and historical data. Meanwhile, Bluefield or hybrid approaches are also supported, with data load techniques and side-by-side validation approaches. These tools ensure that whether a customer chooses to migrate or re-implement, the path to HPA is well-supported.

Crucially, there is no forced end-of-life date for the previous planning areas, but the writing is on the wall: new IBP features will increasingly be delivered only for HPA after 2025. For instance, some advanced supply planning capabilities are already exclusive to HPA as of 2026. Therefore, SAP encourages customers to plan their move to HPA to stay current and unlock the full value of IBP. The HPA 2026 strategy provides the means (tools and services) to make that move in a controlled and efficient way.

Your Strategic Call to Action: Embrace HPA for Future-Ready Planning

As an IBP customer, you’re at a pivotal moment. The Harmonized Planning Area (HPA) isn’t just another upgrade—it’s your pathway to unlocking faster value realization, improved performance, and exclusive access to cutting-edge IBP capabilities that will define the future of integrated business planning.

Why This Matters to Your Business

The reality is clear: While there’s no forced end-of-life date for your current planning areas, new IBP features will increasingly be delivered exclusively for HPA after 2025. Advanced supply planning capabilities are already HPA-exclusive as of 2026.

With HPA, you gain:

  •              Complete end-to-end planning visibility across all modules
  •              Lower total cost of ownership with streamlined integration
  •              Access to the latest innovations that won’t be available in legacy planning areas

Your Path Forward: Choose What Works for You

New to IBP or Ready for a Fresh Start?

Go Greenfield – Start with HPA from day one. New IBP installations default to HPA as of the 2508 release. You’ll be aligned with SAP’s strategic roadmap from the beginning.

Running Live IBP Systems?

Take the Brownfield Approach – Use 2025 to experiment with HPA in sandbox environments while migration tools like MOVE2H mature. Plan your controlled transition for 2026-2027 when full tooling support is available.

Want Maximum Control?

Consider Bluefield – Run HPA in parallel to your existing planning areas, allowing you to redesign and harmonize processes at your own pace while maintaining business continuity.

Immediate Steps You Should Take

  1. Assess Your Current State Today
  •              Identify your specific migration requirements and timeline
  1. Get Expert Support
  •              Join the Early Adopter Care Program – Get direct access to SAP product experts starting with the IBP 2508 release
  •              Connect with SAP consultants or certified partners for your HPA roadmap
  1. Start Learning Now
  •              Access updated documentation on the SAP Help Portal
  1. Plan Your Strategy
  •              Align your approach with the 2026-2027 tool availability roadmap
  •              Consider running pilot projects in sandbox environments

Don’t Wait – The Competitive Advantage is Real

Companies moving to HPA now will benefit from:

  •              Enhanced Functional Scope
  •              Simplified maintenance and reduced complexity
  •              Better positioning for future IBP innovations
  •              Improved planning performance across your entire supply chain

Ready to Begin?

Your journey to HPA success starts with a single step. Contact your SAP account team for personalised guidance on your HPA adoption strategy.

The future of integrated business planning is here. Make sure your organisation captures its full value.

entry.title: Event Recap — SAP Solutions for PLM Info Days
entry.content:
🌟 Event Recap  of SAP Solutions for PLM Info Days 2026 —How SAP Is Enabling Connected, Customer‑Driven Product Innovation

For three days, experts from engineering, R&D, manufacturing, quality, compliance, IT, and project management came together to explore one of the most pressing questions facing modern product organizations:

How do we build products faster, smarter, and more responsibly in an increasingly complex world?

Each day examined this question through a different lens — discrete industries, process industries, and project & portfolio management — yet a shared storyline ran across all discussions. Attendees repeatedly highlighted challenges around fragmented product data, heavy compliance workloads, disconnected processes, and rising expectations for AI-enabled efficiency.

Across the event, one thing became clear: Integrated, intelligent PLM is no longer optional. It’s becoming the operational backbone of modern product organizations.

🔍 Three Cross‑Event Themes That Defined the Week

1. Product data fragmentation remains the biggest barrier

Whether teams manage CAD files, formulation data, specifications, or project plans, many participants described workflows that still depend on disconnected tools, inconsistent handovers, and manual reconciliation. These issues slow development and create risks long before a product reaches the shop floor.

2. Handover friction is still one of the costliest issues

Across industries, attendees shared how difficult it remains to move product definitions cleanly from engineering → manufacturing, formulation → production, and planning → execution. Every time a document, recipe, product structure or bill of materials changes hands, teams lose time, context, or accuracy.

This was one of the clearest signals from the entire event: handovers matter as much as the work itself.

3. AI is shifting from potential to practice

Perhaps the biggest mindset shift was the acceptance of AI as a practical, embedded partner in everyday workflows. Across the three days, participants responded strongly to examples of AI:

  • identifying risks earlier
  • supporting change impact analysis
  • assisting formulation decisions
  • forecasting project schedules
  • enhancing decision-making with natural-language insights

The enthusiasm showed that organizations are ready for AI that is useful, contextual, and integrated directly into their PLM workflows.

🛠️ What Resonated Across the Three Days

Even though each day focused on a different domain, attendees gravitated toward similar ideas — all centered on simplifying complexity and strengthening the flow of product data.

🔧 Day 1 — Discrete Industries

Conversations centered on the importance of engineering data that flows: CAD, product structures, visualization, governed change management, and supply-chain-ready definitions. Engagement grew whenever the link between engineering and manufacturing became seamless and traceable.

🧪 Day 2 — Process Industries

Participants in process industries highlighted the complexity of connecting formulation, specification management, labeling, and compliance. Reactions showed strong interest in structured property management, automated labeling, collaboration scenarios, and native integration with downstream processes.

📊 Day 3 — Portfolio & Project Management

Project and portfolio leaders focused on predictability: aligning resources, forecasting risks, synchronizing plans with execution, and managing ETO complexity. Interest peaked whenever real-time data and AI provided clarity in situations that are normally opaque.

🌐 Bringing It All Together — A Future Defined by Flexibility

Across all three days of the SAP Solution for PLM Info Days 2026, one insight rose above everything else: there is no single PLM journey. Every organization attending navigates a different mix of tools, processes, partners, and regulatory requirements — and SAP’s strategy recognizes and embraces that diversity.

Discrete manufacturers wrestle with CAD-heavy engineering environments; process industry teams manage formulations, specifications, and compliance; and project-driven organizations balance resources, risks, and constantly shifting priorities. Yet despite these differences, a consistent truth connected every conversation:

Product data only delivers value when it is connected — and each customer needs the freedom to define how that connection happens.

That is why SAP’s solutions for PLM approach centers on choice. Rather than prescribing a rigid architecture, SAP empowers customers through three complementary paths:

  • Direct CAD Integration
     For engineering-led companies that want design files, product structures, and change processes flowing cleanly into the SAP Business Suite.
  • Cloud-Native Product Data Management
     For teams looking for collaborative capabilities on SAP BTP — formulation, specification governance, product structures — fully integrated with SAP’s operational backbone.
  • PLM System Integration
     For organizations invested in external PLM, enabling bi-directional synchronization and smooth orchestration of product data across hybrid landscapes.

What ties these paths together is the SAP Business Suite — the system of record that anchors product truth, unifies supply chain and execution processes, and unlocks operational value from engineering and R&D decisions.

The strongest takeaway from the event was not about any single tool or capability. It was about empowerment. Customers want to modernize on their own terms, evolve their PLM landscape without disruption, and integrate what works today with what they want tomorrow. SAP’s portfolio — from SAP Engineering Control Center and SAP Integrated Product Development on SAP Business Technology Platform to SAP Project & Resource Management, SAP Enterprise Portfolio & Project Management, and PLM System Integration — gives them exactly that freedom.

✨ Final Thoughts — How This Blog Series Fits Together

This overall recap, along with the three deep-dive blogs for discrete industries, process industries, and project & portfolio management, forms a complete picture of SAP’s flexibility-first PLM strategy.

Each blog explores a different entry point into the product lifecycle, but together they show how SAP enables companies to build their own path toward a connected, intelligent, lifecycle-driven product development ecosystem.

Rather than forcing customers into a single definition of PLM, SAP provides a flexible foundation — grounded in the SAP Business Suite, enhanced by cloud innovation, and extended through open integration — so every organization can design a PLM journey that fits their industry, their maturity, and their future.

Continue Your Journey with SAP solutions for PLM

If you missed a session or want to revisit highlights, you can still watch the SAP Solutions for PLM Info Days 2026 on‑demand.

We’ll continue deep‑diving into engineering, formulation, compliance, project management, and AI‑enabled product development throughout the year. Make sure to bookmark our upcoming webinars to stay ahead of the latest innovations.

To stay connected with the PLM community of SAP:

  • Follow us on LinkedIn for insights, updates, and customer stories
  • Join the SAP Community and follow the Product Lifecycle Management tag
  • Explore the latest product tour on sap.com/plm to see how our solutions help streamline product data and accelerate innovation

Thank you for joining us at SAP Solutions for PLM Info Days 2026 — we’re excited to continue shaping the future of connected intelligent product development with you.

entry.title: Your Blueprint to Lead the Shift in SAP S/4HANA Utilities
entry.content:
The Utilities industry is moving fast toward cloud-ready architectures, customer-first operations, and intelligent,  AI-enabled processes. If you're an architect, business user , or consultant, you're at the center of this transformation.  

The journey begins with a thorough introduction to SAP S/4HANA Utilities, covering unique system capabilities, end-to-end industry processes, and customer service operations. From there, you will explore the strategic differences between SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA Utilities, learning to evaluate conversion options and apply proven transition methodologies using tools like SAP Readiness Check. 

The curriculum then guides you through essential system configuration, including master data modeling, device management with inventory and procurement processes, meter reading, and integration with other SAP components. Billing and invoicing are addressed in depth, from rate structure configuration to budget billing plans and exception handling. 

For the German energy market, specialized training covers market communication processes, including the cloud-based SAP Market Communication (MaCo) solution, Application Process Engine (APE), and the integration into SAP S/4HANA Utilities with a focus on regulatory compliance and automation. 

Finally, the curriculum prepares you to build future-proof architectures by keeping the SAP S/4HANA core clean through extensibility on SAP Business Technology Platform, and utilizing SAP Fiori for a modern, industry-specific user experience. 

Ready to go hands on? 

Build end-to-end skills with the refreshed course stack:  

  1. S4IUT is your overview and entry point to the SAP S/4HANA for Utilities. The course covers  the business processes , Fiori-based functions, AI-enabled optimization, asset management architecture, and cloud integrations: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/executing-business-processes-in-sap-s-4hana-utilities 
  2. IUT21 focuses on master data and basic functions configuration: https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-master-data-and-basic-functions-in-sap-s-4hana-utilities 
  3. IUT22 deep-dives into device management configuration, from master data and inventory to installations, inspections, and meter reading: https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-device-management-in-sap-s-4hana-utilities 
  4. IUT23 covers billing and invoicing configuration, including rate structures, execution, exceptions, and budget billing: https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-billing-and-invoicing-in-sap-s-4hana-utilities 
  5. If you operate in the German energy market, IUT51 explores market communication (MaCo-Cloud) with the Application Process Engine (APE): https://learning.sap.com/courses/explore-sap-market-communication-for-utilities-in-the-german-energy-market 
  6. Round it out with the updated S4IUD gives you the strategic and technical insights to guide customers from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA with confidence: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/transitioning-to-sap-s-4hana-for-utilities 

Ready to lead your customers’ transition with clarity and impact? Start with S4IUT (or S4IUD), then progress through the updated stack to build execution and configuration expertise.

entry.title: Re: Fiori Elements list report controller extension types...
entry.content:
Try this:

 

import ControllerExtension from 'sap/ui/core/mvc/ControllerExtension';
import ExtensionAPI from 'sap/fe/templates/ListReport/ExtensionAPI';
import XMLView from 'sap/ui/core/mvc/XMLView'
/**
 * @namespace com.jk.apps.accountwfts.ext.controller
 * @Controller
 */
export default class GeneralDataList extends ControllerExtension<ExtensionAPI> {
	static overrides = {
		/**
		 * Called when a controller is instantiated and its View controls (if available) are already created.
		 * Can be used to modify the View before it is displayed, to bind event handlers and do other one-time initialization.
		 * @memberOf com.jk.apps.accountwfts.ext.controller.GeneralDataList
		 */
		onInit(this: GeneralDataList) {
			const view = (this.base.getExtensionAPI().getRouting().getView() as XMLView);
		}
	}
}

entry.title: Trying to understand SAP in simple terms? Ever wondered what SAP actually is?
entry.content:
In this episode of SAP Learning Insights, David Chaviano hosts Bob Szymanski, a university professor from Georgia Southern University and a prominent voice in integrating SAP into academic curricula. The conversation is about the fundamental question: What is SAP? David reveals his desire to present SAP in a beginner-friendly manner, and Bob takes this opportunity to share his insights, experiences and easy to understand examples. Bob presents a clear, straightforward definition of SAP, describing it not merely as software, but as an enterprise information system that enables business processes.  He shares sources for getting more knowledge and insights and at the end, he of course provides his final words of wisdom.

Download transcript

Dive deeper

  • Bob Szymanski on LinkedIn
  • SAP
  • Bob's book on Enterprise Systems
  • Student Zone on learning.sap.com
  • SAP University Alliances (SAP Community)
  • Learn how students can certify in SAP for free with SAP learning hub, student edition 
 

Further resources

  • Visit SAP Learning 
  • Visit the SAP Community 
 

Enjoy listening to this new podcast! 

Apple          Spotify

entry.title: Is It Possible to Set a Custom Temp Directory for lcm_cli Migration?
entry.content:
I have performed a clean installation of SAP BusinessObjects 4.3 SP3 on Linux, and I need to migrate content from the current 4.2igration, lcm, pmw SP5 environment.

I initially tried to use the Promotion Management Wizard (PMW), but it does not work correctly. The process starts but then doeigration, lcm, pmws nothing: it neither migrates the content nor generates any logs. This issue seems to be consistent with what is described in the following SAP Community thread:
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-q-a/migration-issues-sapbo-4-2-to-4-3-pmw-error-and-missing-relationships-with/qaa-p/14254183#M4930229

For this reason, I am performing the migration using lcm_cli.sh, split into three phases, each executed with its own script:

  • users
  • dependencies
  • content

The users and dependencies scripts run successfully. However, I am encountering an issue with the content migration: the available space in the default temporary directory is limited, and I do not have enough capacity in that path (it requires approximately 2.5x the size of the FRS).

Is it possible to specify an alternative temporary directory, either via a modifier/parameter in the script itself or in the command used to launch lcm_cli.sh, so that a custom temporary path can be used?

entry.title: Re: Migrating to Seamless Planning in SAP Analytics Cloud: Steps, Pitfalls, and Workarounds
entry.content:
@N1kh1l 

At this time Data Actions built against Seamless Planning models using the Cross Model Copy Actions only support connecting to other Seamless Planning models and vice versa. If you want to copy data from a model persisted in SAC to a model persisted in Datasphere you would need to use another mechanism. For example, using the Data Import/Data Export services in a Python script within Datasphere, etc. or an ODATA Data Import step as part of a Multi-Action. 

Sean

entry.title: Re: Unable to delete auto populated activities in test case
entry.content:
Hi, Vishakha,
You mention a screen shot, but it didn't save in your post.

I checked what I think you are doing in Cloud ALM Test Preparation and believe I understand your steps and the problem you are questioning.  Here is a screen shot from my review showing my original manually created test case where I later assigned a Solution Process which loaded its activities to the test case.

When I click Delete, only the manually created activities offer the option to remove the steps:

If this is correct, this is by design, per the built-in help:

which states:

Delete Activities

To delete an activity, choose Delete and then choose (Delete) next to the activity.

Please note that you can only delete custom activities that were manually created. If you want to exclude an activity that is derived from a solution process flow diagram, remove it from the scope of the test case instead.

--

If I missed your point, do share further detail.

Best regards,
Paula

entry.title: Old Tile Still Appearing in SAP Build Work Zone After Complete Re‑Exposure from S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I am facing an issue with federated content from an on‑premise S/4HANA system to SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition).
Our authorization team has removed the old tile and assigned a new tile to the correct custom role, custom catalog, and custom group in S/4HANA.

To ensure a clean setup, I deleted the previous exposure configuration and re‑created the full setup from scratch in:

  • /UI2/CDM3_EXP_SCOPE (content exposure)
  • SAP BTP → Build Work Zone → Content Provider → Synchronize Content

Despite repeating the full process multiple times, the old tile continues to appear in Build Work Zone instead of the new one.
The authorization consultant has verified both the new role and the tile assignments several times on the S/4HANA side, and everything looks correct there — but Build Work Zone keeps federating only the old tile.

Has anyone experienced something similar?

I am trying to understand:

  • Why outdated tile information is still being federated
  • Whether additional cleanup is required on the CDM or content provider side
  • If there are any cache layers (S/4HANA, BTP, or Build Work Zone) that must be explicitly cleared
  • Whether Exposure Version 2 requires specific steps to refresh or replace previously published app IDs

Any advice, known issues, or guidance on how to force Build Work Zone to pick up the updated tile would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: Re: Joule Studio: How to Create Document Grounding
entry.content:
@Julia_Kraus16 Are you asking or telling me about pipelines, or has there been a change I wasn't aware of?

With this method of creating document grounding, all you need to supply is a destination to an AI Core and to a Document Store. SAP Build (Joule Studio) will handle:

  • Creating the Resource Group
  • Adding the Generic Secret (include all that Base64 stuff)
  • Create a pipeline for the documents in your Document Store

All the stuff you have to do for document grounding in AI Core, still needs to be done but Joule Studio will do it for you. Of course, currently you cannot reuse an existing pipeline you created manually, but I am told that is coming.

entry.title: Re: Joule Studio: How to Create Document Grounding
entry.content:
@nicholas_nicolis I am sorry I missed your question from a while ago. I am assuming you created a Document Grounding in the Control Tower but your agent is not able to get information from it.

First, it's important to make sure agent is built the best it can be so that it always recognizes when to use the Document Grounding tool you added. 

Second, assuming the first is OK, sometimes Document Grounding has not worked for me. To be honest, I haven't found a good answer for that. I often reload the project, stop and restart the testing, and it will eventually work, but I'm not sure what is going on.

Third, you must consider that something is wrong with AI Core, which has had some periods of instability over the last month.

entry.title: Re: Claude Code via MCP: Poor man's Joule, or a practical tool?
entry.content:
Does anybody know in which cases the local code is synchronized with SAP?

Sometimes it makes changes directly in SAP without even touching the local file.

And then when you open a new session, it can just take the local file (which is no more relevant), do the changes in it and send it to SAP, causing inconsistencies.

Any ideas, how to make sure the local code is always up-to-date? Maybe there is a way of forcing it to download the code from SAP, everytime a new session is opened?

entry.title: Withholding Tax Reporting Date - Wrong Date after payment run
entry.content:
Dear Support Team,

I am currently facing an issue with the base date for the withholding tax declaration for vendor invoices where withholding tax is applied.

In the example attached, I posted an invoice with withholding tax applied at the time of posting. As you can see, the document base date is 02.02.2026, which is correct. However, when I run the Automatic Payment Program, this date is automatically changed and set to the date when the payment run is executed. As a consequence, the tax declaration for form 111 becomes incorrect.

The correct behavior should be that if the withholding tax is posted in February, it should appear in the same month of February in the declaration.

I am attaching screenshots of the invoice posted with withholding tax before and after the payment, as well as the payment proposal:

Payment Proposal

 

 

Posting after payment proposal

entry.title: Depreciation Key for Intangible Assets (No 50% reduction in the first year)
entry.content:
Hi,

I am currently working on an SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud implementation for an Italian company and I am facing a localization issue regarding Asset Accounting.

My client raised a specific requirement regarding the depreciation of Intangible Assets (e.g., software, licenses, patents). According to Italian tax law, intangible assets should be depreciated without the 50% reduction in the first year of acquisition, which is instead mandatory for tangible assets.

Currently, the standard depreciation keys provided for Italy (such as the "I**" series) seem to apply the 50% reduction (half-year convention) in the first year by default.

Since we are on Public Cloud and we do not have full access to create or deeply modify depreciation keys and period controls from scratch (like we used to do via AFAMA in On-Premise), my questions are:

  1. Is there a specific standard SAP Depreciation Key for Italy designed explicitly for Intangible Assets that calculates the full quota without the 50% first-year reduction?

  2. If not, what is the best practice or the specific SSCUI / Configuration Activity to adjust the period control method to meet this Italian legal requirement?

Thanks in advance for your support!

Best regards,
Andrea

entry.title: Re: sac planning model data export from export jobs
entry.content:
The easiest way to do this is to create an ODATA connection on the backend DB and PULL the data from the SAC Model. You can configure an API Subscription to export only DELTAs in the Data as well. More information on these APIs and API Subscriptions can be found here:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/00f68c2e08b941f081002fd3691d86a7/52688092e7b74c229e42d41f2dc4c898.html?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=LATEST 

I know for certain that Datasphere supports connecting to SAC using this method and I suspect that Oracle will as well.

The other approach for Datasphere is to look at migrating the model to Seamless Planning. This will persist the SAC Plan data on the Datasphere tenant making all of the Plan data accessible via Datasphere views. There are a number of blogs on the SAP Community which discuss Seamless Planning which you may want to check out.

Sean

entry.title: Re: SAP Service and Asset Manager Phase model Work Orders
entry.content: I dont think there is any restriction to phase or non phase in the clock in/out for an operation. Do you have any other operations already in started state? this is normally the reason you cant clock into a second operation on the device. the concept been that a technician can only work on one operation at a time. so they should put a current operation on hold or complete before starting work on a new operation.

entry.title: Re: BTC - (Business Transformation Cycle) step sequence u...
entry.content:
@MoepMoep Hi Moe,

the correct answer is 2)

Or if Customizing steps (1) should be done before BTC Post-Processing step and Data migration steps (2) after BTC Post-Processing step?

You can prepare the target shell system upfront with all relevant customizing, best before you start the overall migration cycle since you might now want to perform this during the system downtime anyhow.

Then after BTC Post Processing is completed, you need to perform the SFIN conversion as you have explained via the IMG.

This is the link to our docu with best practices: https://help.sap.com/docs/btc/lean-sdt-best-practices/overview

The part about SFIN is here: https://help.sap.com/docs/btc/lean-sdt-best-practices/sap-finance-in-sap-s-4hana-conversion

Regarding the right sequence, I suggest you create a transformation project for scenario Selective Data Transition which has an exact playbook/steplist including all the steps: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-business-transformation-center-is-now-part-of-the-integrated-toolchain/ba-p/14075484

The app transformation project overview then gives a great insight into the actual migration steps.

Hope this helps.

Matthias

entry.title: Is SAP ABAP developer still a good career option in 2026 for someone starting fresh in IT?
entry.content:
Hey everyone,

I'm a 22-year-old fresh CSE graduate who's been job hunting for the past 4–5 months in software/web development. I've applied to a lot of openings, got a few calls, but my performance in coding rounds has been consistently poor — and I want to be honest about why.

I understand concepts well. Ask me about theory, system design fundamentals, or how something works under the hood — I'm comfortable. But when it comes to actually writing code from scratch under pressure, I freeze. I can't translate what I know into working solutions fast enough.

On top of that, I'm fairly introverted. My English is fine when I'm relaxed or by myself, but in interviews I fumble — and I think a big part of that is low confidence coming from feeling underprepared technically.

Watching batchmates land jobs while I'm still searching has been hard. I want to be able to contribute to my family, and the longer this drags on, the more I feel like I'm falling behind.

So I've been doing research, and I'm seriously considering pivoting to SAP ABAP as a career path. A few specific things I'd love honest opinions on:

  • Is SAP ABAP worth learning in 2026 for someone starting fresh?
  • How relevant will it stay in the AI era — or will it get replaced?
  • I've read that adjacent areas like SAP BTP and SAP Fiori have stronger long-term prospects. Is that accurate?
  • Would this path give someone without strong DSA/competitive coding skills a realistic entry point into a stable, well-paying tech career?

I'd really appreciate perspectives from people who work in SAP or have a broader view of where enterprise tech is headed. Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Re: Lock Parent vs Leaf Members
entry.content:
You can also Lock the Parent manually using the context of the Table object in the Design view. This will persist at runtime, however I find that this approach works best IF you set the Table for Mass Data Entry, otherwise every change of one of the Leaf members will cause all of the other Leaf members to recalculate.

I used this exact approach to calculate the Adjustment Node of an ORG and PRODUCT dimension. I set the Adjustment Node to Read Only and then Lock the parent cell. When you enter data into one of the other Leaf Nodes it will go into Mass Data Entry mode. You can continue entering data for multiple cells and then when you click on the Process Data button the adjustment node will be recalculated so that all of the Leaf nodes add up to the Parent.

To make this approach truly seamless I did have to add some API calls to check for UNCHANGED values and then programmatically set these values to *1 so that they included the changed value flag otherwise any UNCHANGED value would also get updated during the Post Data event. I can provide more details on how to make this work if this is the kind of user experience that you are looking for.

Sean

entry.title: SAP Reference Architecture: Share your feedback!
entry.content:
Are you working with SAP Reference Architecture on a regular basis? We’d love to hear your thoughts and experiences! Join this Customer Engagement Initiative and take part in regular feedback rounds, kicking off with an initial project call in April. Share with SAP’s Product Managers what experiences you have made with SAP Reference Architecture and its representation within the integrated tool chain, especially in SAP LeanIX and SAP Signavio.

📅Registration is open until this Friday, March 13, 2026.

About SAP Reference Architecture
The SAP Reference Architecture provides a harmonized reference business and solution architecture which aims to align business and IT by mapping business capabilities and processes to IT solutions, enabling Enterprise Architects to leverage this content in SAP LeanIX to define their baseline/target application landscape. Instead of building solutions from scratch, organizations can apply SAP Reference Architectures to guide decisions and align projects.

As part of a series of 56 projects, this Customer Engagement Initiative project from the first cycle in 2026 offers SAP customers and partners the opportunity to collaborate directly with SAP teams, to shape the next generation of SAP solutions. Its planned format is a regular call of about one hour, with additional deep dives as needed or requested.

We are excited to hear from you!

______________________________________________________________
➡️Sign up to this CEI project on SAP Reference Architecture using this link ⬅️

entry.title: Released CDS View for Sales Price Condition Records with Material ID, in S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

System : SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

We are building an external OData API in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.
Our goal is to merge Sales Pricing data with Inventory (Material Stock)
data into a single API for downstream consumption.

GOAL:
Expose a combined Stock + Sales Price API by joining:
- I_MaterialStock_2 (inventory/stock levels per plant & storage location)
- A Sales Price CDS view (with Material ID as the join key)

PROBLEM:
The only relevant released CDS view we found is I_SlsPrcgConditionRecord
— but it does NOT include the Material / Product field.
Without Material ID, we cannot join pricing data with stock data
at the material level.

ALREADY CHECKED:
- I_SlsPrcgConditionRecord — no Material ID field
- I_SlsPrcgConditionRecordValidity — Material ID not consistently exposed
- SAP API Business Hub (api.sap.com) — no suitable released OData service found
- SAP Help Portal and SAP Notes — no workaround found

DESIRED API DATA MODEL (illustrative):

SELECT
s.Material,
s.Plant,
s.StorageLocation,
s.MatlWrhsStkQtyInMatlBaseUnit AS StockQuantity,
p.ConditionRateValue AS SalesPrice,
p.TransactionCurrency,
p.ConditionValidityEndDate
FROM I_MaterialStock_2 AS s
JOIN <Missing Sales Price CDS> AS p ON s.Material = p.Material

REQUIRED FIELDS from the missing Sales Price CDS view:
- Material / Product (mandatory join key)
- ConditionType (e.g. PR00)
- ConditionRateValue (price amount)
- TransactionCurrency
- SalesOrganization / DistributionChannel (for filtering)
- ConditionValidityStartDate / ConditionValidityEndDate

QUESTIONS:
1. Does a released CDS view exist that exposes Sales Price
Condition Records WITH Material ID?
2. If not, is SAP planning to release one  and when?
3. What is the SAP-recommended approach to join I_MaterialStock_2
with Sales Price data for an external-facing OData API?
4. If a custom approach is needed, is RAP/BTP extensibility
the right pattern within S/4HANA Cloud boundaries?

We have exhausted all self-service options before posting here.
Any guidance from the community or SAP experts is greatly appreciated!

Thank you.

Rudhra

entry.title: Re: The Bill Note document number is not running accordin...
entry.content:
Dear @Chanaporn,

In Public Cloud for Thailand payment receipt types, SAP can derive the official document number and use it as the External ID, depending on the payment receipt type configuration so if the External ID is not resetting after the new year the likely reason is that the related ODN setup is currently year-independent. SAP supports both Year Independent and Year Dependent Fiscal Year numbering for official document numbers

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/88d44bce214447bcb7cbbdc03eb16aa1/5c0101ead4424d79989170d09b6080f8.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/88d44bce214447bcb7cbbdc03eb16aa1/d79dd2ab94ad49b0a99ff36d9e00928a.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/88d44bce214447bcb7cbbdc03eb16aa1/2326984293c74bdab9c7d6a815c82503.html

In CBC steps to check:

1. Open CBC and search for Define Payment Receipt Types ID 103387

2. Open the Thailand receipt type used for the Bill Note and confirm that the official document number is used as the External ID

3. In CBC review the Official Document Numbering setup and check whether the number range is configured as Year Independent or Year Dependent Fiscal Year. SAP documents that fiscal-year-dependent numbering creates a new set of ranges for each fiscal year

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/60a09f68f2444ceca31dcac2e7017945/acd1c1a68726487981b5621e988b3ac8.html

4. If the business requirement is to restart numbering every fiscal year, maintain the year-dependent intervals in Maintain Number Range for Year Dependent ODN Types ID 106200

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/60a09f68f2444ceca31dcac2e7017945/3db864e4e09e4c14bac83257b1483568.html

Summary:
This is supported in standard but it must be configured in ODN/CBC. The Manage Payment Receipts Thailand app itself does not control the fiscal-year reset logic

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: DDCR™ — The Technology-Agnostic Engine That Eliminates Integration Sprawl in Enterprise Architecture
entry.content:
Introduction

In many enterprise integration landscapes, complexity does not arrive all at once. It grows silently.

A new API is created for one business unit. Another package is added for a second system. A new team introduces its own routing convention. Then another gateway policy appears. Then a different platform. Then a workaround. Then another technical user. Then more duplicated logic.

Over time, what should have been a coherent integration architecture becomes a fragmented landscape of flows, policies, scripts, mappings, and endpoint decisions spread across multiple tools and teams.

That is the problem of integration sprawl endless ploriferation.

In the API Management layer, it appears as API proxy sprawl — often a 1:1 relationship between proxies and backends.

In the orchestration layer, it becomes package or orchestration-object sprawl.

At the same time, organizations face technical user sprawl across both API Management and orchestration platforms.

The result is simple: sprawl everywhere — continuously growing, poorly organized, and increasingly difficult to govern.

With the rapid introduction of AI and LLM-based services, the challenge becomes even greater. The number of integrations, endpoints, and routing scenarios will only continue to expand, making it even harder for enterprises to maintain control over their integration landscape.

I believe there is a way to address this.

What I am proposing is an architectural approach that I am sharing freely for anyone — any developer, any architect, any company, anything — to explore and adopt.

This approach does not replace existing technical platforms or silos. Instead, it sits above them, introducing a semantic domain layer end to end in all objets names and developments, that helps organize whole enterprise architecture landscape

Understanding this model requires a certain level of architectural abstraction beyond vendor manuals, but once understood, it offers a different way of thinking about enterprise integration.

This is where SDIA, with GDCR™ and DDCR™ for API Management platforms, or DDCR™ alone for other technologies, comes into play.

 

What is SDIA?

SDIA stands for Semantic Domain Integration Architecture.

It is an architectural way of thinking about integration in which routing, control, and organization are driven by domain meaning rather than technical fragmentation.
A domain can represent business capabilities, products, countries, cities, hospitals, IoT ecosystems, LLM services, or any other semantic boundary that defines how systems should interact.

Instead of asking:

  • Which gateway product is being used?
  • Which vendor-specific policy should be configured?
  • Which endpoint is hardcoded in this flow?
  • Which package owns this route?

SDIA asks a more important question:

What is the domain intent of this request ?

That shift changes everything.

In SDIA, requests are interpreted through semantic domains samples such as:

Business domains

  • sales
  • orders
  • customers
  • invoices
  • deliveries
  • payments

Industry domains

  • healthcare
  • finance
  • logistics
  • insurance
  • manufacturing

Geographic domains

  • country
  • region
  • city
  • branch
  • distribution center

Organizational domains

  • department
  • team
  • business unit
  • partner network

Technology domains

  • IoT devices
  • AI / LLM services
  • analytics platforms
  • messaging systems
  • event streams

Infrastructure domains

  • cloud provider
  • Kubernetes cluster
  • edge network
  • zero-trust gateway
  • service mesh

Operational domains

  • monitoring
  • security
  • identity
  • audit
  • compliance

The architecture becomes organized around the meaning of the domain operation rather than around scattered technical artifacts.

In simple terms, SDIA is the architectural model that provides end-to-end semantic domain organization. It is composed of GDCR, DDCR, and ODCP. More details about these components can be found in the official DOI documents.

This blog focuses specifically on the DDCR engine, a deterministic semantic routing mechanism based on mathematical principles. In testing, the engine has never failed across approximately ~2,067,873 messages, implemented in five different programming languages and validated across eight technical platforms.

Tools & Technologies Validated

Languages

  • JavaScript (SAP BTP APIM — Target Endpoint PreFlow)
  • Lua (Kong Gateway — custom plugin)
  • Java (Netflix Zuul — DdcrPhantomFilter route filter)
  • Python (AWS Lambda + IoT MQTT publisher)
  • C# (Azure API Management — policy inbound)

API Gateways & APIM

  • SAP BTP API Management
  • Kong Gateway (standalone)
  • Kong Gateway on Kubernetes
  • Netflix Zuul (Spring Cloud, Java)
  • AWS API Gateway + Lambda
  • Azure API Management

Orchestration & Backend

  • SAP BTP Integration Suite (CPI)

KVM / Metadata Stores

  • SAP KVM (native BTP)
  • Redis (standalone local)
  • Redis on Kubernetes (Docker Desktop)
  • In-memory HashMap (Java static)

Infrastructure & Containers

  • Kubernetes (Docker Desktop, namespace sdia)
  • Istio Service Mesh
  • Docker (Redis, Kong, Node-RED, Mosquitto)

IoT Stack

  • Mosquitto MQTT Broker
  • Node-RED
  • Python MQTT publisher (42 sensors, 4 environments)

DDCR defines the semantic control logic that brings order, consistency, and determinism to enterprise integration.

 

What is DDCR?

DDCR stands for Domain Driven Centric Router.

DDCR is the routing engine that can operate inside the SDIA model.

If SDIA is the architectural vision, DDCR is the component that executes the routing decision.

DDCR reads the business intent of a request, extracts the semantic domain, constructs a deterministic routing key, consults metadata, resolves the real destination, and dispatches the request to the correct target.

 

 

DDCR inside GDCR

For SAP where I started this development APIM and CPI, everything is ready over this repository but you need to read the documents to understand, if you don't do that, don't go you will waste your time , after that maybe 10 min if you have APIM experience : 

https://github.com/rhviana/sdia/tree/main/gdcr-proven/gdcr-drcp-sap-api

Within a broader GDCR architecture, DDCR sits in the routing/control layer.

It is the component responsible for the deterministic routing decision.

That means DDCR is not the entire architecture by itself. It is one core component of the larger control model.

However, DDCR is also powerful because it can be used independently.

It does not need to exist only inside GDCR.

It can be applied in isolation in any environment that needs semantic routing and deterministic destination resolution.

For example, DDCR can be implemented in:

  • API Management platforms ( Target End-Point — PREFLOW)
  • Kubernetes ingress layers
  • service meshes
  • reverse proxies
  • zero-trust gateways
  • event brokers
  • IoT gateways
  • LLM gateways
  • middleware runtimes
  • serverless routing layers

This is one of its strongest characteristics:

DDCR is technology-agnostic and platform-agnostic.

It is not locked to a vendor. It is not locked to a product. It is not locked to a gateway.

It can run anywhere a request can be intercepted and routed.

DDCR, in turn, acts as the deterministic routing engine, responsible for resolving the semantic intent of a request and directing it to the correct destination based on metadata.

Instead of spreading routing logic across multiple flows, scripts, and platform-specific rules, routing decisions become deterministic, metadata-driven, and domain-oriented.

GDCR governs the APIM integration landscape, while DDCR executes the routing decision.

SDIA™
├─ GDCR™ (governance + control architecture — API Management)
│
├─ DDCR™ (deterministic routing engine — (Anything)
│
└─ ODCP™ (Semantic Domain Topology of Naming Convention over any objects regards the integration in the cloud or on-premise middleware layer for any vendor)

 

Before the three examples below, I would like to give you an internal view of how the DDCR engine works.

I am the sole architect, engineer, and independent researcher (Brazilian) responsible for the design, formalization, documentation, releases, and implementation of this engine and the broader Semantic Domain Integration Architecture (SDIA).

The core architectural concepts were conceived and formalized with all documents, git and others within 32 days.

I have already received feedback from a respected expert in the field with contributions dating back to 2003.

Comming back to DDCR:

It operates based on deterministic domain semantics — essentially applied mathematics.

The principle behind it is completely technology-agnostic, just like mathematics.

2 + 2 = 4 and 5 + 5 = 10 or 16–6 = 10 anywhere in the world — regardless of the technology, vendor, platform, country, company, hardware, infrastructure, programming language, language ( English, Portuguese, Russian, Chinese, Japonese) all the same result.

In the same way, DDCR applies deterministic semantic logic that produces the same routing decision anywhere it is implemented.

Because the logic is mathematical, the engine behaves consistently across any technology stack.

In DDCR, routing decisions are not based on technical artifacts, scripts, or platform-specific rules.They are based on interpreting intent and resolving it deterministically through semantic domains.

As you read the following examples, please pay close attention to one thing above all:

the intention of the person making the request.

The bookstore analogy: the easiest way to understand the DDCR enginne

Imagine a person walking into a bookstore.

A student walks into a library and approaches the librarian.

→ “Good morning, I’d like to borrow a mathematics book.”

His intent: /library/borrow/books/mathematics

The librarian has worked there for 50 years. Listens. Consults a notebook with a key

booksmathematicg01

Responds:

→”Of course — your book is in the mathematics section, first floor.”

 

Target Result: /books/section/mathematics/firstfloor

Student — “Thank you , Im going there now, section, mathematics at firsfloor”

The hospital analogy: visiting the dentist

Imagine a person walking into a hospital.

A patient arrives at the reception desk and speaks to the hospital receptionist.

→ “Good morning, I need to see a dentist.”

His intent: /hospital/visit/dentist

The receptionist has worked there for many years. She listens carefully and understands the intent.

She consults the hospital routing notebook, which contains deterministic keys.

dentistvisitg01

The receptionist immediately knows where the patient must go.

She responds:

→ “Of course — the dental department is on the third floor, room 305.”

Target Result: /hospital/dental/department/thirdfloor/room305

Patient:

→ “Thank you, I’m going there now — dental department, third floor, room 305.”

 

The airport analogy: finding the correct gate

Imagine a person arriving at a large international airport.

A traveler walks to the information desk.

→ “Good morning, I need to catch my flight to Tokyo.”

His intent: /airport/travel/tokyo

The airport agent has access to the airport flight routing system. She listens carefully and identifies the destination.

She checks the flight information system using a routing key.

flighttokyog01

The system immediately returns the correct routing information.

The agent responds:

→ “Your flight to Tokyo departs from Gate A12, Terminal 2.”

Target Result: /airport/terminal2/gateA12/tokyo

Traveler:

→ “Thank you, I’m heading to Terminal 2, Gate A12 now.”

 

How the DDCR Engine Works

The code that I released also in git inside the policies for APIM it's plugin and play, also all keys for KVM is in the repository, the processing time around 2 - 4ms all lines - I tested with SAP APIM and CPI 121.000 ms p55 143ms cross atlantic with SAP BTP Trial, imagine production.

/**
 * ============================================================================
 * GDCR Routing Engine – Phantom v12 (PRODUCTION READY)
 * ============================================================================
 * Author:    Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana
 * Email:     rhviana@gmail.com
 * License:   CC BY 4.0 (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)
 * DOI:       https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18619641
 * GitHub:    https://github.com/rhviana/gdcr
 *
 * Zero regex | Zero hardcode | Zero split | O(1) lookup after warm cache
 * Compatible: SAP BTP APIM (Rhino) | Apigee | Kong (with adapter)
 * ============================================================================
 *
 * KVM key format:  dcrp<process><entity><actioncode><vendor>id<digits>
 * KVM value format: <adapter>
 * Example:
 *   Key:   dcrpfinanceorderscsalesforceid01
 *   Value: http
 *
 * Path format: /<entity>/<action>/<vendor>[/<id>[/<extra>...]]
 * ============================================================================
 */

// ── Canonical Action Map — Phantom v12 ──────────────────────────────────────
// 241 action variants → 15 canonical single-character codes
// Author: Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana
// DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18582492

var GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP = {

  // ── c: CREATE (27 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "create":"c",    "created":"c",    "post":"c",
  "insert":"c",    "add":"c",        "new":"c",
  "submit":"c",    "register":"c",   "registered":"c",
  "provision":"c", "provisioning":"c","onboard":"c",
  "onboarding":"c","publish":"c",    "publishing":"c",
  "generate":"c",  "build":"c",      "built":"c",
  "open":"c",      "opening":"c",    "setup":"c",
  "initialize":"c","initializing":"c","initialized":"c",
  "draft":"c",

  // ── r: READ (25 variants) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  "read":"r",      "get":"r",        "fetch":"r",
  "retrieve":"r",  "view":"r",       "viewed":"r",
  "list":"r",      "listing":"r",    "listed":"r",
  "show":"r",      "showing":"r",    "shown":"r",
  "search":"r",    "searching":"r",  "searched":"r",
  "find":"r",      "finding":"r",    "found":"r",
  "query":"r",     "querying":"r",   "queried":"r",
  "lookup":"r",    "check":"r",      "inspect":"r",

  // ── u: UPDATE (27 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "update":"u",    "updating":"u",   "updated":"u",
  "put":"u",       "patch":"u",      "patching":"u",
  "modify":"u",    "modifying":"u",  "modified":"u",
  "change":"u",    "changing":"u",   "changed":"u",
  "edit":"u",      "editing":"u",    "edited":"u",
  "revise":"u",    "revising":"u",   "revised":"u",
  "amend":"u",     "amending":"u",   "amended":"u",
  "replace":"u",   "replacing":"u",  "replaced":"u",
  "upsert":"u",

  // ── d: DELETE (20 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "delete":"d",    "deleting":"d",   "deleted":"d",
  "remove":"d",    "removing":"d",   "removed":"d",
  "cancel":"d",    "cancelling":"d", "cancelled":"d",
  "terminate":"d", "terminating":"d","terminated":"d",
  "destroy":"d",   "destroying":"d", "destroyed":"d",
  "deactivate":"d","purge":"d",      "purging":"d",
  "purged":"d",    "discard":"d",

  // ── s: SYNC (18 variants) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  "sync":"s",         "syncing":"s",       "synced":"s",
  "synchronize":"s",  "synchronizing":"s", "synchronized":"s",
  "replicate":"s",    "replicating":"s",   "replicated":"s",
  "mirror":"s",       "mirroring":"s",     "mirrored":"s",
  "refresh":"s",      "refreshing":"s",    "refreshed":"s",
  "reconcile":"s",    "harmonize":"s",     "align":"s",

  // ── a: APPROVE (18 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "approve":"a",    "approving":"a",   "approved":"a",
  "authorize":"a",  "authorizing":"a", "authorized":"a",
  "accept":"a",     "accepting":"a",   "accepted":"a",
  "validate":"a",   "validating":"a",  "validated":"a",
  "confirm":"a",    "confirming":"a",  "confirmed":"a",
  "endorse":"a",    "certify":"a",     "signoff":"a",

  // ── n: NOTIFY (18 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "notify":"n",     "notifying":"n",   "notified":"n",
  "alert":"n",      "alerting":"n",    "alerted":"n",
  "inform":"n",     "informing":"n",   "informed":"n",
  "announce":"n",   "announcing":"n",  "announced":"n",
  "broadcast":"n",  "broadcasting":"n","broadcasted":"n",
  "emit":"n",       "trigger":"n",     "signal":"n",

  // ── t: TRANSFER (16 variants) ────────────────────────────────────────────
  "transfer":"t",   "transferring":"t","transferred":"t",
  "send":"t",       "sending":"t",     "sent":"t",
  "move":"t",       "moving":"t",      "moved":"t",
  "migrate":"t",    "migrating":"t",   "migrated":"t",
  "forward":"t",    "forwarding":"t",  "relay":"t",
  "handoff":"t",

  // ── e: ENABLE (12 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "enable":"e",     "enabling":"e",    "enabled":"e",
  "activate":"e",   "activating":"e",  "activated":"e",
  "start":"e",      "starting":"e",    "started":"e",
  "resume":"e",     "resuming":"e",    "resumed":"e",

  // ── b: DISABLE (12 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "disable":"b",    "disabling":"b",   "disabled":"b",
  "deactivate":"b", "deactivating":"b","deactivated":"b",
  "stop":"b",       "stopping":"b",    "stopped":"b",
  "pause":"b",      "pausing":"b",     "paused":"b",

  // ── v: ARCHIVE (10 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "archive":"v",    "archiving":"v",   "archived":"v",
  "store":"v",      "storing":"v",     "stored":"v",
  "backup":"v",     "backing":"v",     "backed":"v",
  "retain":"v",

  // ── w: RESTORE (10 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "restore":"w",    "restoring":"w",   "restored":"w",
  "unarchive":"w",  "recover":"w",     "recovering":"w",
  "recovered":"w",  "rollback":"w",    "revert":"w",
  "reinstate":"w",

  // ── x: AUDIT (12 variants) ───────────────────────────────────────────────
  "audit":"x",      "auditing":"x",    "audited":"x",
  "log":"x",        "logging":"x",     "logged":"x",
  "trace":"x",      "tracing":"x",     "traced":"x",
  "track":"x",      "tracking":"x",    "tracked":"x",

  // ── z: EXECUTE (12 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "execute":"z",    "executing":"z",   "executed":"z",
  "run":"z",        "running":"z",     "ran":"z",
  "process":"z",    "processing":"z",  "processed":"z",
  "compute":"z",    "computing":"z",   "computed":"z",

  // ── f: FLOW/ROUTE (10 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────
  "flow":"f",       "flowing":"f",     "flowed":"f",
  "route":"f",      "routing":"f",     "routed":"f",
  "dispatch":"f",   "dispatching":"f", "dispatched":"f",
  "pipeline":"f"

};

// Validation
var entries = Object.keys(GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP);
var codes = {};
entries.forEach(function(k) {
  var c = GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP[k];
  codes[c] = (codes[c] || 0) + 1;
});
// Total: 241 variants → 15 canonical codes
// ── Worker-scoped caches (survive across requests on same worker) ────────────
var _kvmCacheRaw  = null;   // raw KVM string last seen
var _kvmCacheMap  = null;   // parsed map: {lookupKey -> entry}
var _cpiHostRaw   = null;   // raw host last seen
var _cpiHostNorm  = null;   // normalized host (trailing slash removed)

// ── Char code constants (zero string allocation on comparison) ───────────────
var CC_SLASH = 47;   // '/'
var CC_UNDER = 95;   // '_'
var CC_0     = 48;   // '0'
var CC_9     = 57;   // '9'
var CC_A_LOW = 97;   // 'a'
var CC_Z_LOW = 122;  // 'z'

// ============================================================================
// parseKvm — runs ONLY when KVM string changes (amortized O(1) per request)
//
// Strategy for process extraction (zero hardcode, zero regex):
//   Key structure: dcrp + <process> + <entity> + <actioncode> + <vendor> + id<digits>
//   We know: entity (from request path) — BUT at parse time we don't have entity.
//   Solution: store the stripped key (after removing dcrp prefix and id suffix)
//   and resolve entity/process split at lookup time using the entity from request.
//   This keeps parse O(n) over KVM entries and lookup O(k) over map keys (k<=30).
// ============================================================================
function parseKvm(kvmString) {
  var map = {};
  var len = kvmString.length;
  var start = 0;

  while (start < len) {

    // ── Find entry boundaries ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    var comma = kvmString.indexOf(',', start);
    if (comma === -1) comma = len;

    var colon = kvmString.indexOf(':', start);
    if (colon === -1 || colon > comma) { start = comma + 1; continue; }

    var keyRaw  = kvmString.substring(start, colon);
    var adapter = kvmString.substring(colon + 1, comma);

    if (!keyRaw || !adapter) { start = comma + 1; continue; }

    var keyLow = keyRaw.toLowerCase();

    // ── Extract numeric ID from end of key (e.g. "id01" → "01") ──────────
    var idVal = '';
    var idPos = keyLow.lastIndexOf('id');
    if (idPos !== -1 && idPos < keyLow.length - 2) {
      var dStr = '';
      for (var di = idPos + 2; di < keyLow.length; di++) {
        var dc = keyLow.charCodeAt(di);
        if (dc >= CC_0 && dc <= CC_9) dStr += keyLow.charAt(di);
        else break;
      }
      if (dStr) idVal = dStr;
    }

    // ── Strip 'dcrp' prefix and 'id<digits>' suffix ───────────────────────
    // Result: <process><entity><actioncode><vendor>
    var stripped = keyLow.substring(4); // remove leading 'dcrp'
    if (idVal) {
      // remove trailing 'id' + digits
      stripped = stripped.substring(0, stripped.length - (2 + idVal.length));
    }

    // ── Store entry indexed by stripped key ───────────────────────────────
    // process is resolved at lookup time (we need entity from request path)
    map[stripped] = {
      adapter:     adapter,
      id:          idVal,
      keyOriginal: keyRaw,
      keyLow:      keyLow,
      stripped:    stripped
    };

    start = comma + 1;
  }
  return map;
}

// ============================================================================
// routingDCRP — main entry point, called by SAP APIM JS policy
// ============================================================================
function routingDCRP() {
  var ctx = context;

  // ── Phase 1: Fetch required variables (fail-fast on missing) ─────────────
  var hostInput = ctx.getVariable('target.cpi.host');
  var kvmInput  = ctx.getVariable('kvm.idinterface');
  var pathInput = ctx.getVariable('proxy.pathsuffix');

  if (!hostInput || !kvmInput || !pathInput) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error',
      'Missing:' +
      (hostInput ? '' : ' [target.cpi.host]') +
      (kvmInput  ? '' : ' [kvm.idinterface]') +
      (pathInput ? '' : ' [proxy.pathsuffix]')
    );
    return;
  }

  // ── Phase 2: Normalize host (cached — runs only when host changes) ────────
  if (_cpiHostRaw !== hostInput) {
    _cpiHostRaw = hostInput;
    var hLen = hostInput.length;
    _cpiHostNorm = (hLen > 0 && hostInput.charCodeAt(hLen - 1) === CC_SLASH)
      ? hostInput.substring(0, hLen - 1)
      : hostInput;
  }
  var host = _cpiHostNorm;

  // ── Phase 3: Path parse — zero regex, zero split, manual indexOf ──────────
  // Expected: /<entity>/<action>/<vendor>[/<id>[/<extra>]]
  if (pathInput.charCodeAt(0) !== CC_SLASH) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error', 'Path must start with /');
    return;
  }

  var s1 = pathInput.indexOf('/', 1);
  if (s1 === -1) { ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success','false'); ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error','Missing entity segment'); return; }

  var s2 = pathInput.indexOf('/', s1 + 1);
  if (s2 === -1) { ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success','false'); ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error','Missing action segment'); return; }

  var s3 = pathInput.indexOf('/', s2 + 1);
  if (s3 === -1) { ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success','false'); ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error','Missing vendor segment'); return; }

  var entity = pathInput.substring(1,      s1).toLowerCase();
  var action = pathInput.substring(s1 + 1, s2).toLowerCase();
  var vendor = pathInput.substring(s2 + 1, s3).toLowerCase();

  // Strip optional leading underscore from vendor
  if (vendor.charCodeAt(0) === CC_UNDER) vendor = vendor.substring(1);

  // Optional: id and extra path segments
  var s4 = pathInput.indexOf('/', s3 + 1);
  var id        = (s4 === -1) ? pathInput.substring(s3 + 1) : pathInput.substring(s3 + 1, s4);
  var extraPath = (s4 === -1) ? '' : pathInput.substring(s4);

  if (!entity || !action || !vendor) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error', 'Empty path segment');
    return;
  }

  // ── Phase 4: Action normalization — O(1) hash lookup, char fallback ───────
  var actionCode = GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP[action];
  if (!actionCode) {
    var c0 = action.charCodeAt(0);
    actionCode = (c0 >= CC_A_LOW && c0 <= CC_Z_LOW) ? action.charAt(0) : 'x';
  }

  // ── Phase 5: KVM cache — rebuild only when raw string changes ─────────────
  if (_kvmCacheRaw !== kvmInput) {
    _kvmCacheRaw = kvmInput;
    _kvmCacheMap = parseKvm(kvmInput);
  }

  // ── Phase 6: Lookup — O(k) over map, k <= 30, ~0.05ms ────────────────────
  // Build lookup pattern: <entity><actioncode><vendor>
  // This matches the middle section of stripped key: <process><entity><actioncode><vendor>
  var lookupPattern = entity + actionCode + vendor;
  var match = null;
  var matchStripped = null;

  var mapKeys = Object.keys(_kvmCacheMap);
  for (var mi = 0; mi < mapKeys.length; mi++) {
    var mk = mapKeys[mi];
    if (mk.indexOf(lookupPattern) !== -1) {
      match        = _kvmCacheMap[mk];
      matchStripped = mk;
      break;
    }
  }

  if (!match) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error', 'No route for: ' + lookupPattern);
    return;
  }

  // ── Phase 7: Process extraction — fully dynamic, zero hardcode ───────────
  // stripped = <process><entity><actioncode><vendor>
  // We know entity from request path → find its position → process = everything before it
  var process = 'unknown';
  var eIdx = matchStripped.indexOf(entity);
  if (eIdx > 0) {
    process = matchStripped.substring(0, eIdx);
  } else if (eIdx === 0) {
    // No process prefix — domain-only routing, process = entity
    process = entity;
  }

  // ── Phase 8: Build target URL ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  var url = host + '/' + match.adapter +
            '/dcrp/' + process +
            '/' + entity +
            '/' + actionCode;

  if (match.id) url += '/id' + match.id;
  if (id)       url += '/' + id;
  if (extraPath) url += extraPath;

  var qs = ctx.getVariable('request.querystring');
  if (qs) url += '?' + qs;

  // ── Phase 9: Write output variables (success path only) ───────────────────
  // Optional vars fetched here — not on fail paths (saves bridge calls)
  var packageName = ctx.getVariable('kvm.packagename') || 'unknown';
  var sapProcess  = ctx.getVariable('kvm.sapprocess')  || 'unknown';
  var messageId   = ctx.getVariable('messageid')       || '';

  ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success',           'true');
  ctx.setVariable('target.url',                      url);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-process',  process);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-adapter',  match.adapter);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-version',  '20.0');
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-key',      match.keyOriginal);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-packagename',   packageName);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-sapprocess',    sapProcess);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-senderid',      vendor);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-correlationid', messageId);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-idinterface',   match.id);
}

// ============================================================================
// PERFORMANCE PROFILE (SAP BTP APIM / Apigee Rhino engine)
// ============================================================================
// Cold start (first request, cache miss):  ~3-4ms (parseKvm runs once)
// Warm requests (cache hit):               ~1.5-2.5ms
// KVM size supported:                      up to ~200 entries without degradation
// Memory per worker:                       <5KB (map + strings)
// Regex operations:                        ZERO
// Array split operations:                  ZERO
// Hardcoded entity/process patterns:       ZERO
// Object allocations per warm request:     ~3 (url string, mapKeys, loop var)
// ============================================================================

At its core, DDCR follows a 7-stage deterministic routing pipeline. Zero regex. Zero hardcode. Zero split. Pure mathematics.

Stage 1 — Domain Inference The engine intercepts the incoming request and parses the semantic URL using manual indexOf — no regex, no split.

Stage 2 — Policy Execution AuthZ and Zero Trust boundary enforcement. The request identity is validated before any routing logic executes.

Stage 3 — Route Guard (Fast Fail — M2M) If the semantic URL is malformed or the intent is invalid, the engine rejects immediately at ~0.1ms. The backend never receives an invalid request.

Stage 4 — Normalize The action verb is canonicalized. 241 variants map to 15 codes.

Stage 5 — Build KVM Key The engine constructs the deterministic metadata key.

[prefix][entity][actionCode][vendor][flowId]:[adapter]

dcrp · orders · c · Salesforce · id01 : http

→ dcrporderscSalesforceid01:http

Stage 6 — Resolve O(1) lookup against the KVM store. Compatible with Redis, DynamoDB, HashMap, Kubernetes ConfigMap, PostgreSQL, etcd — any key-value store.

/sales/orders/create/salesforce
→ dcrporderscSalesforceid01:http
→ https://cpi-host/http/dcrp/orders/c/id01

Stage 7 — Dispatch The request is forwarded to the resolved backend. The consumer URL never changes — ever.

Consumer: POST /sales/orders/create/salesforce ← permanent
Backend: https://cpi-host/http/dcrp/orders/c/id01 ← resolved

In one sentence:

The engine converts semantic intent into a deterministic routing key, resolves it via O(1) KVM lookup, and dispatches — while fast-failing any invalid intent before it reaches the backend.

 

Intent → Inference → Policy → Guard → Normalize → Key → Resolve → Dispatch

Conclusion

DDCR is not a framework. Not a library. Not a vendor product. It is a mathematical invariant — a governing principle that emerges from the nature of enterprise integration itself.

As SDIA is not product, no, it's not a product it's a high abstract enterprise integration architecture based on semantic domains, in the background vendors, groovy script, jdbc, xslt, java script, soap, rest bla bla bla bla but what is the domain intention ? This is end of sprawl worldwide.

The proof is not theoretical. It is empirical.

~2,067,873 requests. Zero routing failures. Sub-4ms latency. 99.99% uptime. Validated across 8 platforms, 5 programming languages, and every major integration layer — APIM, orchestration, Kubernetes, service mesh, IoT edge, and AI gateway — under constrained conditions, using free-tier trial accounts, local Docker environments, and community editions. No production budget. No team. No vendor support.

One engineer. One invariant. One result.

The four publications establishing prior art are registered on Zenodo under open DOIs, protected under USPTO Trademark Application Serial , and indexed in the IP.com Prior Art Database:

SDIA Governing architecture umbrella 10.5281/zenodo.18877636

IP — IPCOM000277630D

GDCR Semantic gateway facade 10.5281/zenodo.18836272

IP — IPCOM000277632D

DDCR Deterministic routing engine 10.5281/zenodo.18864833

IP — IPCOM000277633D

ODCP Orchestration naming convention 10.5281/zenodo.18876594

IP — IPCOM000277631D

Github — Free with all details, use, download, implement all codes everything is there, still under construction in some areas:

https://github.com/rhviana/sdia

All publications are released under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0).

The architecture is intentionally open and vendor-neutral. Any engineer, organization, or system in the world is free to study, implement, extend, and build upon it — including for commercial purposes.

No fees, royalties, or permissions are required, no money required, is FOR FREE ! Just read and plugin and play on SAP APIM and CPI test it, you will see reduction of SPRAWL in 90%.

Help ? Send me message, I replay when I have time, I'm doing last documentations and github.

The only requirement of the CC BY 4.0 license is proper attribution to the original author when the work is publicly referenced or used.

What is means ? The whole architecture concept SDIA ( GDCR + DDCR + ODCP ) is protected all concepts, details codes etc..etc..and FREE TO USE anywhere.

The entire architecture concept SDIA (GDCR + DDCR + ODCP) is publicly documented and released as prior-art.

This publication exists specifically to ensure that the architecture remains free for the world, preventing proprietary ownership, vendor lock-in, or patent enclosure by corporations or big tech.

Any engineer, company, or system may implement, extend, and build upon it.

The only requirement is attribution to the original author.

The goal is simple: keep the architecture and all components open forever.

The authorship is not open. It is permanent forever, as domains the same forever.

I don't want anything, I'm fine and looking for new challanges and that's it.

The semantic domain boundary is platform-agnostic, protocol-agnostic, language-agnostic, and vendor-agnostic by mathematical definition. Any system that routes, resolves, or governs integration artifacts — regardless of the technology stack — is operating within the space that SDIA was designed to govern.

The domain never lies.

Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana, March 2026 https://orcid.org/0009-0009-9549-5862

entry.title: Re: How to combine a pop-up result with onBeforeExecute?
entry.content:
The easiest way to achieve this is to use the Data Action Scripting object. Then you can use your own custom button to launch the Popup and execute the Data Action. You will have to link the Data Action parameters to Input Controls or manually pass them in via scripting, but this is definitely the easiest approach if you want to have more control over the Data Action itself. So you would have something like this:
Button_1.onClick():

Popup_1.open();
if(ScriptVariable_Proceed){
	Application.showBusyIndicator('Running Data Action');
	var response = DataAction_1.execute();
	if(response.status === DataActionExecutionResponseStatus.Success){
		Application.showMessage(ApplicationMessageType.Success, 'Data Action completed successfully');
	}else if(response.status === DataActionExecutionResponseStatus.Error){
		Application.showMessage(ApplicationMessageType.Error, 'Data Action failed');
	}
	Application.hideBusyIndicator();	
}else{
	Application.showMessage(ApplicationMessageType.Info, 'Data Action will not run');
}

Popup_1.onButtonClick():

if(buttonId==='ok'){
	ScriptVariable_Proceed=true;

}else{
	ScriptVariable_Proceed=false;
	
}
this.close();

Of course you can always trigger the Data Action directly from the Popup.onButtonClick() event as well.
